text.title: Tracking Plan Rules and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Defines the closed set of tracking rules for event naming, cohort comparison, dashboard lifecycle, and LLM-specific failure modes in analytics workflows.

rule.statement: Use object_action format in snake_case for all event names (e.g., account_created, feature_used, upgrade_started); reject any event name that does not follow this pattern.
rule.rationale: Without a strict naming convention, teams end up with signup_done, account_created, and user_registered all meaning the same thing; the funnel fragments silently across three tables.

rule.statement: Freeze the event taxonomy in git (tracking-plan.md); runtime catalogs drift toward it via PR, never the other way. Run a weekly cron drift-linter comparing the live event catalog against tracking-plan.md.
rule.rationale: Engineers ship new events without updating the tracking plan; the cron linter is the only sustainable fix — agents that are not constrained to the taxonomy in their system prompt invent event names that fragment the funnel.

rule.statement: Every event row must state which decision it informs; reject any event without a stated decision at tracking plan review time.
rule.rationale: "Track everything" sprawl is the primary driver of analysis paralysis and dashboard graveyards; decision-first requirements eliminate events nobody will ever query.

rule.statement: Compare D7-of-cohort-A to D7-of-cohort-B (fixed window); never compare D30 vs D7 across cohorts.
rule.rationale: Comparing D30 retention of a 6-week-old cohort to D7 of a 1-week-old cohort makes the older cohort look worse by design; readers misread it as a retention regression.

rule.statement: Exclude cohorts that have not yet aged to the measurement window from retention calculations; survivorship bias systematically over-states retention for fast-growing products.
rule.rationale: An agent computing D30 retention for all users including those who signed up less than 30 days ago will produce a number that looks like retention but is actually just "users who have had time to churn."

rule.statement: Apply a kill-criterion to every dashboard: archive if no opens in 30 days; enforce via an agent-driven cron check.
rule.rationale: Agents create dashboards easily and never archive them; without a kill-criterion the dashboard inventory grows monotonically until no PM can find the one they actually need.

section.title: Agent Gotchas

antipattern.description: Agent inventing event names (e.g., user_signed_up) when the codebase uses signup_completed.
antipattern.reason: Always pass the canonical event taxonomy in the agent system prompt and reject novel event names; hallucinated event names create schema gaps that are invisible until a dashboard shows zero data.

antipattern.description: Agent SQL joining events to users to sessions without DISTINCT, producing triple-counted metrics.
antipattern.reason: Naive joins fanout rows; require count(distinct user_id) and gate queries via sqlfluff rules, or use a metrics layer (dbt-metrics, Cube) so agents cannot write raw fanout SQL.

antipattern.description: Agent providing a single confident cause when asked "why did retention drop?"
antipattern.reason: Funnels and cohorts answer "what" and "where," never "why"; force the agent to list 3+ candidate causes and the disambiguating query, never a single explanation without a cited experiment.

antipattern.description: Agent including raw event payloads (with emails, IPs, free-text) in LLM prompts.
antipattern.reason: Product events leak PII; always aggregate or hash before any LLM call; run a password-scrubber-agent pass on any data before it enters a prompt context.

section.reference: Enforce snake_case naming and required fields on the tracking plan markdown
